plainEnglish: Visual structure—headings, lists, tables, form labels, grouped fields—must be expressed in the markup, not just with styling. If something looks like a heading, use an <h2>; if it's a list, use <ul>/<li>; associate every form input with a <label>. That way screen readers can convey the same structure that sighted users see.
whyItMatters: Screen reader users navigate by headings, lists, and labels; when structure is faked with CSS instead of semantic HTML, they can't understand relationships or jump around the page.
quickCheck: Open the Chrome DevTools Accessibility tree (or run axe DevTools) and confirm headings, lists, tables, and form labels are real semantic elements, not styled <div>s.
commonMistakes:
Styling a
or

with big, bold text to look like a heading instead of using

–

.
Building a list out of <div>s and line breaks instead of <ul>/<ol> and <li>.
Using a placeholder as the only label for an input instead of an associated <label>.
Laying out tabular data with <div>s, or a <table> with no <th> headers, so cell relationships are lost.
Grouping radio buttons or checkboxes with no <fieldset> and <legend>.
